coredata.dc:title: Boosting photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater by synergy effect of heterojunction and phosphorus doping
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jie
Ding, Jing
Wan, Hui
Guan, Guofeng
coredata.dc:description: Phosphorus-doped g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 (PCN/ZIS) heterojunction photocatalysts were constructed by solvothermal method. The physical and chemical properties were investigated with X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), etc. The degradation of antibiotic wastewater was used to investigate the photocatalytic activities of composites under visible-light irradiation. The 10% PCN/ZIS had the best photocatalytic degradation performance for tetracycline with a photodegradation rate of 0.0874 min−1, which is respectively about 2.9 and 52.0 times than that of pure ZIS and PCN. Meanwhile, it was concluded that the holes and ⋅O2 – (superoxide radicals) play dominant roles in the photocatalytic reactions through radicals trapping experiments, while ⋅OH (hydroxyl radicals) has a negative effect. In addition, 10%PCN/ZIS, with excellent stability and recyclability, also exhibited high photocatalytic activity for terramycin, chlortetracycline and ofloxacin. Overall, with the enhanced photocatalytic performance, PCN/ZIS could be potentially applied for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnIn2S4 nanoflower
P-doped g-C3N4
Heterojunction
Antibiotic wastewater
Photocatalytic mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of PCN, ZIS and x%PCN/ZIS with local magnification shown in the inset.
XPS spectra of 10%PCN/ZIS: (a) P 2p; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) Zn 2p; (e) In 3d; (f) S 2p.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PCN, ZIS and x% PCN/ZIS. (b) The band gaps (Eg) of PCN and ZIS.

(a) Photocatalytic performance curves of TC over PCN, ZIS and x%PCN/ZIS; (b) Plot of -ln(C/C 0) against reaction time in the degradation of TC under visible light irradiation; (c) Photocatalytic performance curves of other antibiotics over 10%PCN/ZIS; (d) The rate constant of photocatalytic performances of antibiotics over 10%PCN/ZIS.
The degradation rate constants of TC over the 10%PCN/ZIS in presence of various scavengers.
Schematic representation of the synthesis process of PCN/ZIS photocatalyst.
The possible routes of photoelectron generation and transport in the PCN/ZIS photocatalyst under visible-light irradiation.
Elemental contents of PCN/ZIS by ICP-OES measurement.

head.ce:title: Boosting photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater by synergy effect of heterojunction and phosphorus doping

Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Inorganic Function Composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphorus-doped g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 (PCN/ZIS) heterojunction photocatalysts were constructed by solvothermal method. The physical and chemical properties were investigated with X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), etc. The degradation of antibiotic wastewater was used to investigate the photocatalytic activities of composites under visible-light irradiation. The 10% PCN/ZIS had the best photocatalytic degradation performance for tetracycline with a photodegradation rate of 0.0874 min−1, which is respectively about 2.9 and 52.0 times than that of pure ZIS and PCN. Meanwhile, it was concluded that the holes and ⋅O2 – (superoxide radicals) play dominant roles in the photocatalytic reactions through radicals trapping experiments, while ⋅OH (hydroxyl radicals) has a negative effect. In addition, 10%PCN/ZIS, with excellent stability and recyclability, also exhibited high photocatalytic activity for terramycin, chlortetracycline and ofloxacin. Overall, with the enhanced photocatalytic performance, PCN/ZIS could be potentially applied for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, reports of antibiotics detected in water bodies, indicating that the new environmental pollutants represented by antibiotics have been paid more and more attention [1–2]. The earth's environment is facing greater pollution of antibiotic-containing water because of the discharge of wastewater containing high concentration of antibiotics, the maternal discharge of antibiotics which are extensively used in human, animal husbandry and aquaculture industry after they are not absorbed [3]. Antibiotics can lead to environmental risks such as biological toxicity and resistance genes of pathogenic bacteria, which have seriously endangered the ecological environment and the healthy development of human society [4]. On the other hand, there is no environmental standard for antibiotics. Therefore, how to formulate the environmental standard of antibiotics from the root causes and eliminate the abuse of antibiotics is imminent. In view of the existing pollution situation, to seek efficient and green degradation technique has become a hot spot of scientific researchers' attention [5–8].

Traditional antibiotic wastewater treatment technologies mainly include physical treatment, biological treatment and chemical treatment [9]. These methods generally have problems such as incomplete degradation, strong dependence on antibiotic type and water quality and low degradation efficiency. As a new technology in chemical treatment, photocatalytic technology has been poured great attention into owing to its advantages of degrading organic pollutants, no secondary pollution and low energy consumption. More and more scientists use photocatalytic technology to study the photodegradation of antibiotic wastewater. Xie et al. [10] fabricated a Z-scheme photocatalyst CQDs-MoO3/g-C3N4, which showed highly photocatalytic activity towards tetracycline (TC). Guo et al. [11] used an in-situ precipitation method to synthesize AgI/CuBi2O4 and AgBr/CuBi2O4 heterojunctions. When they were used to degrade TC, the photocatalytic activity of two types of heterojunctions has been significantly quite higher than the composite of pure CuBi2O4. Shi et al. [12] prepared CQDs-CoO by choosing carbon dots to modify and stabilize the (111) facets of CoO. When the content of CQDs was 5%, the co-catalyst could degrade about 87% of TC in 1 h and remained high activity after 5 times of use. However, the core issue of photocatalytic technology is the design of highly efficient catalysts.

Recently, ZnIn2S4 (ZIS), as a narrow bandgap semiconductor photocatalyst, with appropriate band structure, unique photoelectric properties and excellent photochemical stability, has more and more applications in the field of photocatalysis [13]. Gao et al. [14] probed the degradation effect of ZnIn2S4 on typical antibiotics such as tetracycline hydrochloride, chloramphenicol, rifampin, lincomycin hydrochloride and erythromycin. These results showed that the five antibiotics had been completely degraded within 90 min. Tu et al. [15] synthesized nanosheet-constructed ZnIn2S4 microwires with about 10 μm in length without any template or surfactant. Its possible growth mechanism had been revealed. Due to the wide absorption range and the highly exposed (006) facets, the prepared microwires had high photocatalytic degradation effect on different organic pollutants such as dyes, antibiotics and 2,4,6-tribromophenol. In addition to ZnIn2S4, another non-metallic graphite phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN) material can also exhibit visible light response characteristics with low cost, adjustable chemical composition, suitable band structure and chemical stability. It has excellent performance in the fields of photocatalytic hydrogen production [16–17], photocatalytic degradation [18–19], photocatalytic reaction [20–21] and bioimaging [22]. It has been one of the main research hotspots in the territory of photocatalysis. However, in order to overcome the defects of pure CN, for instance, small specific surface area, wide band gap, easier recombination of photo-generated carriers, chemical doping and building heterostructures are carried out.

In this article, a highly efficient heterojunction catalyst P-g-C3N4/ZnIn2S4 (PCN/ZIS) were successfully constructed through a hydrothermal method using phosphorus doping CN and ZIS (Scheme 1 ). The intrinsic effects of the P-doping and the ZIS loading to CN on the structure and photocatalytic activity of PCN/ZIS was explored by a series of characterization methods, such as XRD, TEM, XPS and DRS. The photocatalytic activity of PCN/ZIS was evaluated by the visible light degradation performance of TC and other antibiotics. Moreover, the electron transfer pathway and the possible photocatalytic mechanism over PCN/ZIS photocatalysts were also discussed in detail.

Dicyandiamide (99%), (NH4)2HPO4 (99%) and zinc acetate dihydrate (99.9%) were of analytical grade and were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Inc. Ethanol (analytical reagent 99.7%) was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Corp. Indium nitrate hydrate (99.9%), thioacetamide (99%) and cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) were of analytical grade and were obtained from Aladdin Industrial, Inc. All chemicals can meet required purity for analysis which can be used directly.

PCN was prepared on the basis of thepreviousstudies method with certain modification [23]. In details, 10 g dicyandiamide and 0.6 g (NH4)2HPO4 were dissolved in a certain amount of water. After about 1-hour magnetic stirring, the mixture was dried to remove the excess solvent completely. Subsequently, the acquired solids were heat-treated in a muffle furnace. After treated at 450 °C for 3 h and 550 °C for 3 h, a pale yellow PCN powder was acquired.

PCN/ZIS catalysts were implemented by one-step hydrothermal method. More concretely, 50 mL ethanol, re-dispersed with a certain amount of PCN was treated ultrasonically for about 1 h. Then Zn, In and S with a molar ratio of 1: 2: 8, using zinc acetate dihydrate as the source of zinc, indium nitrate hydrate as the source of indium, thioacetamide as the source of sulfur and 0.1 g cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) were added to the above suspension. After ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, the above mixture was reacted in a stainless-steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene at 180 °C for 10 h. The product was then centrifuged, washed with ethanol and deionized water, and dried in vacuum at 60° C for 24 h. The as-prepared PCN/ZIS heterojunctions with different PCN contents were labelled as × % PCN/ZIS, where × % is the content of PCN in the PCN/ZIS heterojunctions composites.

Agilent 700 Series inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used to determine the elemental contents of PCN/ZIS. Power X-ray diffraction (XRD, BrukerD8 Advance X-ray diffractometer) was taken to test the crystal structure with Cu Kα radiation (1.5404 Å). The 2θ scan range was from 10° to 70°. Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F (RH) Field Emission Electron Microscope) was taken to observe the morphology of the samples. X ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250XI) measurements were performed to analyze the surface composition of the samples with Al Kα radiation at 150 W. The UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was measured using a UV–Vis spectrum (Perkin Elmer, Lambda 650 s) from 200 to 800 nm using polytetrafluoroethylene as the background.

10 mg L−1 TC solutions containing 100 mg photocatalysts were used as target degradation material for photocatalytic performance testing under visible-light irradiation. The reaction process was as follows: TC solutions with photocatalysts were stirred continuously for 10 min without light illuminating to attain adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Afterwards, a 300 W xenon lamp (PerkinElmer Co.) was turned on to obtain a visible-light source by using a UV cutoff filter (λ greater than 400 nm). During the reaction period, about 3 mL samples were taken out every 5 min. After removing the solid matter by high-speed centrifugation, the absorbance of the clarified solution was determined by a UV-1800 spectrometer at the maximum absorption wavelength of 356 nm. The concentrations of TC solution before and after photoreaction were indicated by the symbols C 0 and C, respectively.

The elemental contents of PCN/ZIS were shown in Table 1 . The excess of S in raw materials was chosen to promote the formation of ZIS. In the synthesis process, the excess S was removed after water washing and alcohol washing. The results revealed that the final molar ratio of zinc, indium, and sulfur was 1:2.1:4.1 within experimental error, which was consistent with the stoichiometric composition of ZnIn2S4.

Fig. 1 Showed the powder XRD patterns of the CN, PCN, PCN/ZIS, ZIS nanocomposites. For CN and PCN, two quintessential characteristic diffraction peaks appeared at 13.4° and 27.6°, associated to the (100) and (002) planes of the graphite-like structure of graphitic structure of C3N4 [16,24,25]. For pure ZnIn2S4, three distinctive diffraction peaks appeared at 21.6°, 27.7°, and 47.4° matched well with the (006), (102), and (110) crystal planes of hexagonal phase ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS 65–2023), respectively [26–28]. The area ratios of peak (112) to peak (102) of ZIS was ca. 0.62, which was in agreement with the literatures reported previously [29]. Furthermore, no other peak belonging to impurities such as ZnS, In2S3 were found in XRD patterns, demonstrating that the samples had high purity with single phase. Furthermore, all of the quintessential characteristic peaks of ZIS appeared in the x%PCN/ZIS (x = 1, 5, 10, 20) composites, which indicated that the crystal structure of ZIS was not affected in the hydrothermal process of x%PCN/ZIS heterojunctions. There were no diffraction peaks attributable to PCN in the patterns of all PCN/ZIS, which is mainly attributed to the distance between the characteristic peaks of PCN (27.6°) and ZIS (27.7°) was so close that they overlapped with each other [30]. Besides, with the raise of PCN content in ZIS, the peak of ZIS at 27.7° had a small angle migration.

HRTEM/TEM was used to observe the morphology of 10% PCN/ZIS heterojunctions and the images were manifested in Fig. 2 . As displayed in Fig. 2 a, it revealed that the 10%PCN/ZIS composite was composed of about 1 μm diameter nanoflower that was well dispersed on C3N4 nanosheets. In addition, the SAED pattern (Fig. 2 a inset) depicted two diffraction rings related to (102) and (112) crystal faces of 10% PCN/ZIS, which was compatible with the XRD analysis results of ZIS [29]. Fig. 2 b and Fig. 2 c exhibited that ZIS with a thickness of about 6–8 nm were attached onto the surfaces of PCN nanosheets to form heterojunction structures. As presented in Fig. 2 d, two different kinds of lattice fringes of ZIS were appeared. Corresponding to the (102) and (100) crystal plane of hexagonal ZIS, the lattice spacings were determined to be 0.32 and 0.33 nm, respectively [29]. The TEM result demonstrated that the combinations of PCN and ZIS was so close that the heterojunctions were indeed formed, which was beneficial to the photoinduced interface charge-transfer of PCN and ZIS. Besides, the crystal structure of ZIS was well retained after C3N4 introduced.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectra of PCN/ZIS were suggested in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3 a, the P 2p binding energy peak of PCN/ZIS was centered at ca. 133.6 eV. This was a quintessential peak of PN coordination, demonstrating that phosphorus was likely to replace carbon in CN to form PN bond [25,31–33]. For C 1 s region (Fig. 3 b), two positions located at 284.5, 285.6 eV for PCN were corresponded to the CC and/or adventitious carbon and the CNC of PCN, respectively [34]. Fig. 3 c manifested that the binding energies of PCN/ZIS in N 1 s region were located at 399.4, 400.5 and 401.5 eV, corresponding to sp2 hybrid aromatic nitrogen atoms (C–NC), tertiary nitrogen atoms (N–(C)3) and nitrogen atoms in hydrogen bound amino groups (CNH) with structural defects, respectively [34–37]. The binding energies of PCN/ZIS in Zn 2p region were showed in Fig. 3 d. Two peaks located at 1044.6 and 1021.6 eV of PCN/ZIS in Zn 2p region were relevant to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively [27,29,38,39]. For In3d spectrum of PCN/ZIS (Fig. 3 e), two peaks located at 452.4 eV (In 2p3/2) and 445.1 eV (In 2p5/2) could be assigned to In3+ [13,38,39]. In the asymmetric curve of S 2p (Fig. 3 f), the two deconvoluted peaks at 161.4 eV and 162.6 eV were corresponded to the characteristic S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 for S2−, respectively, which were assigned to S2− [30,39–41]. Since the binding energies of above elements were coincided with those of corresponding orbits reported in literatures, the existences of P, C, N, Zn, In and S were confirmed in as-synthesized samples.

The changes in the electronic properties of samples can be studied by UV–Vis diffuse analysis, and the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was manifested in Fig. 4 . For PCN, the visible-light absorption edge was at ca. 478 nm with slightly blue shift compared with pure CN [42], corresponding to an estimated band gap of 2.62 eV. The result was compatible with the theoretical calculation result of electronic structure of PCN using first-principles [43]. Compared with the band gap about 2.70 eV of undoped CN, the band gap was slight reduced for PCN. This meant that more visible light was absorbed by PCN to enhance photocatalytic activity. According to the formula E= (αhν)1/2, the band gap of ZIS was 2.53 eV [28]. After PCN and ZIS were combined, the band gap of PCN/ZIS become larger than that of pure ZIS, which contributes to the decrease in the recombination rate of photocarriers.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the degradation behaviour of antibiotics in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation was studied by using PCN/ZIS with varying PCN content as photocatalyst. Fig. 5 a revealed the photodegradation curve of TC with time in the presence of different as-synthesized samples. After phosphorus doping, the decomposition rate of TC was slightly up, which was only 7% after 16 min in the case of pure CN. In general, PCN/ZIS heterojunctions represented higher photocatalytic activities to TC than pure CN, PCN and ZIS. Compared with pure ZIS, the photodegradation efficiency of photocatalysts increased with the increasing of PCN content in the heterojunctions. when the PCN content in PCN/ZIS was 10%, the photocatalytic activity was the highest, and proximately 97% of TC was decomposed. Nevertheless, when the PCN content continued to increase, the degradation efficiency of TC decreased.

Kinetics-experiments were taken to study the degradation efficiency of TC over PCN/ZIS photocatalysts, and the results were manifested in Fig. 5 b. On the base of experiments, the kinetics equation could be expressed as follows:
(1)
- ln ( C /
C 0
) = k t + b
where C 0 represents the beginning TC concentration (mg L−1), C represents the instantaneous concentration (mg L−1) at reaction time t (min), k represents the rate constant (min−1), and b represents the intercept. It was obvious that all the kinetic equations were linear, indicating that the degradation process followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The obtained rate constants of PCN, ZIS, 10%PCN/ZIS under visible light irradiation were 0.0017, 0.0296 and 0.0874, respectively.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activities of 10%PCN/ZIS toward other antibiotics, terramycin, chlortetracycline and ofloxacin were also used. As shown in Fig. 5c, 10%PCN/ZIS showed higher photocatalytic activities to TC and ofloxacin than that to terramycin and chlortetracycline with all the degradation efficiency greater than 85% and the rate constants greater than 0.05 min−1 (Fig. 5d). This indicated that PCN/ZIS had good adaptability to various antibiotics.

Fig. 6 displays the TC photodegradation performance in a cycling sequence of photocatalytic runs. After six cycles, the degradation rate of TC over 10%PCN/ZIS remained above 96%, illustrating the excellent stability of 10%PCN/ZIS.

The free radicals trapping experiments were carried on to determine the active species dominating in the process of TC photodegradation. In these experiments, isopropanol (IPA) acted as hydroxyl radical (•OH), triethanolamine (TEA) act as hole (h+) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) act as superoxide radical (•O2−) scavenger. Fig. 7 revealed that the photocatalytic activity of 10%PCN/ZIS was almost unchanged after IPA was added, which indicated that the hydroxyl radicals were not the main oxidative species for PCN/ZIS. However, the degradation curve was obviously changed when TEA and BQ were introduced into the photocatalytic system, indicating that both hole and superoxide radical were the major oxidative species for TC degradation over the PCN/ZIS photocatalysts. With the existence of TEA and BQ, the holes were captured and a great number of electrons combined with O2 to translate to •O2−, which can decompose TC to small molecule.

The band edge potentials of pure PCN and CN were estimated by Mulliken electronegativity equation. The CB and VB edge potentials of pure PCN and ZIS were calculated by Eg and Mulliken electronegativity equation. The calculated values were 1.53 and −1.09 eV for PCN, while the counterpart values were 1.58 and −0.95 eV for ZIS, respectively. The reaction mechanism and the electron transfer pathway diagrams were presented in Scheme 2 . The photogenerated electrons were inspired from the VB of PCN after light irradiation, and could be injected in the CB of ZIS, whereas the creation of the positive holes on VB of ZIS also directly transfer to the VB of PCN. It was attributed to that the E CB and E VB of PCN were more negative than ZIS. Thus, TC was oxidized into photodegradation products by a larger number of electrons that remain on the PCN surface. This was mainly ascribable to the greatly reduced probability of electron-hole recombination. Moreover, the residual h+ on the VB of PCN also plays an important role in the oxidation of TC.

In conclusion, with ethanol as solution, the PCN/ZIS heterostructures were successfully synthesized using P and ZIS as co dopant by solvothermal method. The photodegradation rate of TC on as-synthesized 10% PCN/ZIS heterostructures was 0.0874 min−1, approximately 2.9 and 52.0 times the performance of pure ZIS and PCN, respectively. The synergistic effect between PCN and ZIS in the PCN/ZIS heterojunctions enhanced the photocatalytic activity, which was beneficial to lower recombination probability of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. For TC degradation over the PCN/ZIS photocatalysts, both hole and superoxide radical were the major oxidative species. This work provides a new way to degrade antibiotic wastewater by designing effective heterojunctions.

Jie Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jing Ding: Visualization. Hui Wan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Guofeng Guan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21878159, 21706131, U19B2001), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (No. BK20181378), the Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province, the Science and Technology Program of Nantong, China (JC2018013, JC2018071, JCZ18041).
